title: Domestic Hot Water Temperature Compliance (Legionella Prevention)
app: BACnet gateway, temperature sensors via Modbus or IoT
spl:
index=building sourcetype="bms:water" system="domestic_hot_water"
| bin _time span=15m
| stats avg(temperature_c) as avg_temp, min(temperature_c) as min_temp by location, sensor_type, _time
| eval compliance=case(
    sensor_type="storage" AND min_temp>=60, "Compliant",
    sensor_type="storage" AND min_temp<60, "NON-COMPLIANT",
    sensor_type="return" AND min_temp>=50, "Compliant",
    sensor_type="return" AND min_temp<50, "NON-COMPLIANT")
| where compliance="NON-COMPLIANT"
| table _time, location, sensor_type, avg_temp, min_temp, compliance
description: Legionella bacteria thrive in water between 20-45°C. Building codes require domestic hot water storage above 60°C and distribution above 50°C. Temperature drops below these thresholds create serious health risks and regulatory violations.
value: Legionella bacteria thrive in water between 20-45°C. Building codes require domestic hot water storage above 60°C and distribution above 50°C. Temperature drops below these thresholds create serious health risks and regulatory violations.
implementation: Monitor hot water storage tank and return line temperatures continuously. Alert immediately on any reading below compliance threshold. Log all temperature data for regulatory audit trail.
visualization: Temperature compliance gauge per system; temperature timeline; non-compliance alert log; regulatory compliance report.
knownFalsePositives: Compliance breaches during planned excursions for cleaning, filter changes, or controlled commissioning.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: BACnet gateway, temperature sensors via Modbus or IoT.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=bms:water`, `sourcetype=bms:bacnet`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor hot water storage tank and return line temperatures continuously. Alert immediately on any reading below compliance threshold. Log all temperature data for regulatory audit trail.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=building sourcetype="bms:water" system="domestic_hot_water"
| bin _time span=15m
| stats avg(temperature_c) as avg_temp, min(temperature_c) as min_temp by location, sensor_type, _time
| eval compliance=case(
    sensor_type="storage" AND min_temp>=60, "Compliant",
    sensor_type="storage" AND min_temp<60, "NON-COMPLIANT",
    sensor_type="return" AND min_temp>=50, "Compliant",
    sensor_type="return" AND min_temp<50, "NON-COMPLIANT")
| where compliance="NON-COMPLIANT"
| table _time, location, sensor_type, avg_temp, min_temp, compliance
```

Understanding this SPL

**Domestic Hot Water Temperature Compliance (Legionella Prevention)** — Legionella bacteria thrive in water between 20-45°C. Building codes require domestic hot water storage above 60°C and distribution above 50°C. Temperature drops below these thresholds create serious health risks and regulatory violations.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=bms:water`, `sourcetype=bms:bacnet`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): BACnet gateway, temperature sensors via Modbus or IoT. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: building; **sourcetype**: bms:water. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=building, sourcetype="bms:water". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by location, sensor_type, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **compliance** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where compliance="NON-COMPLIANT"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Domestic Hot Water Temperature Compliance (Legionella Prevention)**): table _time, location, sensor_type, avg_temp, min_temp, compliance


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search in a known window. Spot-check a few events against the BMS console (for example Niagara, Tridium, or Honeywell) and confirm zones, values, and alarms line up. Verify field names and that the right roles can search the index.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Temperature compliance gauge per system; temperature timeline; non-compliance alert log; regulatory compliance report.
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with heating, cooling, and room comfort before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
